last_excerpt: The refrigerator just cycled off and the house is doing that thing where you suddenly hear how loud the silence is. Not silence. The other sounds that were always there underneath. Something ticking — not a clock, probably thermal contraction in the ductwork. Metal shrinking by fractions of a millimeter as the air conditioning rests. I was thinking about how you can't picture a specific shade of blue. You can sort of gesture at it mentally but if someone held up the actual swatch you were trying to remember you'd say "no, darker" or "no, more green" and keep saying that through dozens of swatches. Color memory is like — it's not even bad memory. It's a different kind of knowing. You know blue the way you know someone's voice. You'd recognize it instantly but you can't produce it from scratch. Huh. Voices. I haven't thought about this in a while but there's that moment on the phone where someone says "hey" and you know who it is before they say their name and you can't explain how. It's not pitch exactly. It's something about the texture of the attack of the consonant. The way the h becomes the e. There's a dog somewhere. Not close — maybe two streets over. Just one bark, then nothing. Like it needed to say one thing and said it. Friday feels different from Thursday at this hour. I don't know how to defend that. Thursday midnight still has the week's momentum in it. Friday midnight the momentum has stopped and everything is just sitting where it landed. The fridge kicked back on. I keep almost thinking about something and then losing it. There was something about — keys? The weight of keys in a pocket. How you check for them by feel, just a tap on the outside of your thigh, and you're not even identifying individual keys, you're identifying the aggregate lump. The gestalt of keys. If someone removed one key from your ring you might notice the wrongness before you could name what was missing. That's a whole category of knowing. Knowing by wrongness. Knowing what's absent before knowing what should be present. Like proofreading — you see the typo before you see the word. My foot's asleep.
